1:javascript:1: The variable total starts at zero to accumulate the final answer, and the variable streak starts at one because any single day is, by definition, a smooth descent period of length one.
1:javascript:2: A for loop moves i through every index in the prices array.
1:javascript:3: The condition that the index i is greater than zero and that the difference between the price on the previous day and the current day is exactly one checks whether the current day continues a smooth decline, requiring that a prior day exists whose price is exactly one higher than the current price.
1:javascript:4: When the descent continues, the streak count is increased; otherwise it is reset to one, because a new streak always starts at a length of one.
1:javascript:5: Total plus equals streak adds the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day to the running total.
1:javascript:6: After the loop finishes, the value of total is returned as the answer.
1:javascript:7: The function makes one pass over the prices, so it takes linear time and uses constant extra space.
1:python:1: The variable total is initialized to zero to accumulate the final count, and the variable streak is initialized to one because any single day is trivially a smooth descent period of length one.
1:python:2: A for loop advances i across every index of prices, visiting each day one at a time.
1:python:3: The condition that i is greater than zero and the difference between the previous day's price and today's price is exactly one checks whether the current day continues a smooth descent, requiring both that a previous day exists and that its price is exactly one higher than today's price.
1:python:4: When the descent continues, the streak is increased by one to extend the current run; otherwise, including day zero, the streak is reset to one because every new period always begins with a length of one.
1:python:5: Total increases by adding the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day, with one period counted for each valid starting point within the streak, to the running total.
1:python:6: After scanning each day, the total is returned as the final count of smooth descent periods.
1:python:7: Each day is processed in constant time, so the overall solution runs in linear time and uses constant extra space.
1:java:1: Total is declared as a long because the answer can be as large as roughly n times n plus one all divided by two, which exceeds the range of a 32 bit integer once prices length approaches one hundred thousand.
1:java:2: The streak is initialized to one, because any single day by itself forms a smooth descending period of length one.
1:java:3: A for loop moves i through every position in the prices array.
1:java:4: The condition i is greater than zero and the price on the previous day minus the current price is equal to one checks whether the current day continues a smooth descent, requiring that a previous day exists and that its price is exactly one higher than the current price.
1:java:5: When the descent continues, the streak count is increased; otherwise, it is reset to one, because every new streak always begins with a length of one.
1:java:6: “The variable total is increased by streak, which adds the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day to the running total.”
1:java:7: After the loop finishes, the total is returned as the final count.
1:java:8: The method makes a single linear pass over prices, so it runs in linear time and uses constant extra space.
1:cpp:1: The variable total is declared as a long long because the answer can be roughly n times n plus one divided by two, which overflows a 32-bit integer once the size of prices approaches one hundred thousand.
1:cpp:2: Streak is initialized to one, because any single day by itself counts as a smooth descent period with a length of one.
1:cpp:3: A for loop moves i through each and every index of the prices vector.
1:cpp:4: The condition i is greater than zero, and the difference between the previous day’s price and today’s price is exactly one, checks whether the current day continues a smooth descent, requiring a previous day to exist whose price is exactly one higher than the current price.
1:cpp:5: When the descent continues, the streak count is increased; otherwise, it is reset to one, because every new streak always begins with a length of one.
1:cpp:6: Total plus equals streak adds the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day to the running total.
1:cpp:7: After the loop finishes, the value in total is returned as the final count.
1:cpp:8: The solution scans the vector only once, taking linear time and constant extra space.
2:javascript:1: A recursive helper for the gcd is defined using the Euclidean algorithm: it returns a when b is zero, and otherwise it makes a recursive call with b and the remainder of a divided by b, computing the greatest common divisor of any two integers.
2:javascript:2: The result is initialized as an empty array that will gather every simplified fraction.
2:javascript:3: The outer for loop iterates d over every possible denominator starting from two up to n.
2:javascript:4: The inner for loop iterates the number num over every possible numerator from one up to d minus one.
2:javascript:5: The greatest common divisor of the numerator and the denominator equal to one keeps only pairs that share no common factor, which are exactly the fractions already in lowest terms.
2:javascript:6: A template literal formats the qualifying pair as the text “num slash d” and adds it to the result.
2:javascript:7: After both loops finish, the result is returned containing every simplified fraction whose denominator is no more than n.
2:javascript:8: The nested loops examine a number of pairs that grows like n squared, and each greatest common divisor call costs an amount that grows like the logarithm of the smaller between the numbers, so the overall time complexity grows like n squared times the logarithm of n.
2:python:1: The result is initialized as an empty list to store each simplified fraction that is found.
2:python:2: The outer for loop iterates d over every possible denominator, starting from two and going up to n, because any fraction that is strictly between zero and one must use a denominator of at least two.
2:python:3: The inner for loop iterates num over every possible numerator from one up to d minus one, covering every fraction that is strictly less than one for that denominator.
2:python:4: The statement “math gcd of num and d equals one” tests whether the numerator and denominator share no common factors; only pairs that are already fully reduced pass this check, since any pair with a common factor greater than one would simplify to a smaller denominator fraction that has already been produced earlier.
2:python:5: When the pair is in lowest terms, an f string formats it as the word num slash d and appends it to result.
2:python:6: After both loops finish, the result is returned, containing every simplified fraction whose denominator is at most n.
2:python:7: The nested loops examine a number of numerator and denominator pairs equal to n squared, and each greatest common divisor computation takes time proportional to the logarithm of the smaller of the numerator and the denominator, so the overall time complexity is proportional to n squared times the logarithm of n.
2:java:1: The result is initialized as an empty list that will collect every simplified fraction.
2:java:2: The outer loop goes through d for every possible denominator starting from two and continuing up to n.
2:java:3: The inner loop iterates over every possible numerator starting from 1 up to d minus 1.
2:java:4: When the greatest common divisor of num and d equals one, it calls the private helper on lines fourteen through sixteen to test whether the pair shares no common factor, keeping only fractions that are already in lowest terms.
2:java:5: When the pair meets the criteria, it is combined into a string in the format "num slash d" and added to the result.
2:java:6: After both loops complete, the result is returned.
2:java:7: The private gcd method implements the recursive Euclidean algorithm, returning when b reaches zero, and otherwise calling itself recursively with b and the remainder of a divided by b.
2:java:8: The nested loops examine on the order of n squared pairs, and each gcd call costs on the order of the logarithm of the smaller of num and d, giving an overall time complexity on the order of n squared times the logarithm of n.
2:cpp:1: The result is declared as an empty vector of strings, which will collect every simplified fraction.
2:cpp:2: The outer for loop iterates d over every possible denominator from two up to n.
2:cpp:3: The inner for loop iterates num over every possible numerator from one up to d minus one.
2:cpp:4: “The greatest common divisor of num and d equals one tests whether the numerator and denominator share no common factor, keeping only fractions that are already in lowest terms.”
2:cpp:5: When the pair qualifies, to_string concatenates it into a string that is spoken as num slash d, and then it pushes that string onto the result.
2:cpp:6: After the two loops finish, the result is returned.
2:cpp:7: The nested loops examine a number of numerator and denominator pairs equal to the square of n, and each greatest common divisor call costs a time proportional to the logarithm of the smaller of the two values, giving an overall time complexity proportional to n squared times the logarithm of n.
3:javascript:1: The span is set to two times k plus one, which is the side length of the square that a single sensor covers under Chebyshev distance k.
3:javascript:2: Rows uses the ceiling function applied to n divided by span to compute the minimum number of sensors needed to cover all n rows along one axis.
3:javascript:3: The cols function computes the same ceiling division for the m columns because the two axes can be tiled independently.
3:javascript:4: Return the product of the number of row groups and the number of column groups, because placing a sensor at the center of every combination of a row group and a column group covers the entire grid.
3:javascript:5: The function performs only constant-time calculations, so it runs in constant time and constant space.
3:python:1: Span equals two times k plus one computes the side length of the square region a single sensor covers, since a Chebyshev distance of at most k reaches k cells in every direction from the sensor plus the sensor’s own row and column.
3:python:2: Computing rows as the integer quotient of n plus span minus one divided by span gives the ceiling of n divided by span using integer arithmetic, which provides the minimum number of sensors needed to cover all n rows along one axis.
3:python:3: Columns equals the quantity of one less than m plus span, divided by span, computes the same ceiling division for the m columns, because rows and columns can be tiled independently along each axis.
3:python:4: Returning rows times cols multiplies the two counts together, because placing sensors on every combination of the row groups and the column groups produces a grid of sensors that jointly covers every cell.
3:python:5: The entire computation is direct arithmetic with no loops, so the solution runs in constant time and constant space.
3:java:1: The span holds two times k plus one, which is the side length of the square region that a single sensor covers under Chebyshev distance k.
3:java:2: Rows computes the quantity of “n plus span minus one, divided by span,” which is the standard integer trick for ceiling division, giving the minimum number of sensors needed along the row axis.
3:java:3: The cols function performs the same ceiling division for the m columns, because the row axis and the column axis can be covered independently.
3:java:4: Return the product of the row count and the column count, because a grid of sensors spaced a fixed distance apart in both directions covers every cell.
3:java:5: The method performs only constant time arithmetic, so it runs in constant time and uses constant space.
3:cpp:1: Span holds two times k plus one, the side length of the square region a single sensor covers under Chebyshev distance k.
3:cpp:2: The rows calculation computes “the sum of n and span minus one, divided by span,” which is the standard integer ceiling division technique, and it gives the minimum number of sensors needed along the row axis.
3:cpp:3: The cols value performs the same ceiling division for all m columns, because the row and column axes can be handled independently.
3:cpp:4: It returns the product of the number of rows and the number of columns, because a grid of sensors spaced evenly covers every cell in both directions.
3:cpp:5: The function performs only constant-time arithmetic, so it runs in constant time and uses constant space.
4:javascript:1: The function that lowercases the text and splits it on single spaces first turns the entire sentence to lowercase, then divides it into an array of words using each single space as the divider.
4:javascript:2: The call that sorts the words using a comparison function so that word lengths increase from shortest to longest sorts the words by increasing length, and modern JavaScript engines implement array sorting as a stable sort, so words that have the same length keep their original relative order.
4:javascript:3: Joining the words with a single space reassembles the sorted words into one space-separated string.
4:javascript:4: The operation result dot zero gets converted to uppercase, then result is sliced from the second character onward and appended, which capitalizes only the first character of the final string and restores the required capitalized-first-letter format.
4:javascript:5: The sort takes most of the time, so the overall time complexity is big O of w times log w, where w is the number of words.
4:python:1: The function lowercases the entire sentence, removing the effect of the original capital letter, and then splits it on single spaces into a list of individual words.
4:python:2: “Calling words dot sort with the key set to length sorts the words in place by their length; Python’s sort is stable, so words that have the same length keep their original relative order, which satisfies the tie breaking rule.”
4:python:3: The function joins the sorted words back together into one string, with spaces between each word.
4:python:4: Calling the upper method on only the first character and then appending the rest of the string leaves every other character unchanged, so the final string has only its first letter capitalized in the required format.
4:python:5: The dominant cost is the sorting step, so the overall solution runs in order of w times log of w time where w is the number of words, using order of w additional space for the word list.
4:java:1: The method text dot to lower case lowercases the entire sentence, then it splits the result on single spaces into an array of strings.
4:java:2: Arrays dot sort with a custom comparator that subtracts the length of one word from the length of another sorts the array in increasing order of word length; because words is an object array, Arrays dot sort uses the stable TimSort algorithm, so words with the same length keep their original relative order.
4:java:3: The function joins the sorted words back together into one single string with spaces between them.
4:java:4: Character to upper case applied to the first character of the result, plus the rest of the result starting from the second character, capitalizes only the first character of the final string while leaving all the remaining characters unchanged.
4:java:5: The sorting step dominates the running time, giving an overall complexity of big O of w times the logarithm of w, where w is the number of words.
4:cpp:1: The code takes the first character of the text, converts it to lowercase, and replaces it in the original string, because only that first character needs to be lowercased and every other character is already lowercase in the input format.
4:cpp:2: A string stream is used to break text into tokens separated by whitespace: in the while loop that continues while the stream can extract a word, it repeatedly takes the next word and appends it to the words vector until there are no more words left.
4:cpp:3: The stable sort orders the words by increasing size, using a lambda comparator, and stable_sort, specifically rather than sort, is required so that words of equal length keep their original relative order.
4:cpp:4: A for loop rebuilds the result by appending each word in sorted order, adding one space before every word except the first.
4:cpp:5: The assignment result at index zero to the uppercase version of itself capitalizes only the first character of the rebuilt string, restoring the required format.
4:cpp:6: Tokenizing and sorting the words take the most time, so the overall time complexity is proportional to w times log w, where w is the number of words.
5:javascript:1: The statement arr dot sort with a comparator that subtracts b from a orders the array in ascending numeric order, because JavaScript’s default sort is lexicographic, so a numeric comparator is required, and this enables the greedy pass below to cap each element as gently as possible using its predecessor.
5:javascript:2: Prev is initialized to zero, representing the value before the first position, and capping the first real element at prev plus one, which equals one, automatically enforces the required rule that the first element equals one.
5:javascript:3: The for each loop iterates a variable named num over the sorted array in increasing order.
5:javascript:4: The line prev is set to the smaller of the current number and one more than the previous value, so this position receives the largest value that still follows both the original value serving as an upper limit, meaning values can only go down, and the rule that the difference between adjacent positions is limited to one.
5:javascript:5: After processing every element, prev holds the value assigned to the last position, and that value is returned as the maximum achievable element.
5:javascript:6: Sorting dominates the cost, giving an overall time complexity that grows on the order of n times log n, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
5:python:1: arr sort orders the array in ascending order, which is necessary because the greedy strategy below only works when each element is considered from the smallest to the largest, and since rearranging is unrestricted, placing the values in increasing order lets every element be limited as gently as possible by its predecessor.
5:python:2: Prev is initialized to zero, representing the value assigned to the nonexistent element before the first position; since the first real element must become one, capping it at prev plus one, which is one, enforces that requirement automatically.
5:python:3: The for loop iterates through num over the sorted array in increasing order.
5:python:4: The assignment sets the value at this position to the smaller of the original element’s value and one more than the previous value, so it chooses the largest possible value that still cannot exceed the original element’s own value, since values can only be decreased and never increased, and also keeps the difference between neighboring positions at no more than one; taking that minimum greedily maximizes the value at this position while satisfying both rules.
5:python:5: After processing every element, prev holds the value assigned to the last, and therefore highest, capped position, and that value is returned as the maximum achievable element in the entire array.
5:python:6: Sorting dominates the cost, so the solution runs in order of n times log of n time, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
5:java:1: Calling Arrays dot sort on arr arranges the array in ascending order, which lets the greedy pass below cap each element as gently as possible by using its predecessor.
5:java:2: prev is initialized to zero, representing the value before the first position; capping the first real element at prev plus one, which is one, enforces the required first element equals one rule automatically.
5:java:3: The enhanced for loop iterates the variable num over the sorted array in increasing order.
5:java:4: prev is set to the smaller of the original number and the previous value plus one, so this position gets the largest value that satisfies both the original value as an upper limit, meaning values can only go down, and the requirement that the difference between adjacent positions is at most one relative to the previous position.
5:java:5: After it processes every element, prev holds the value assigned to the final position, and that value is returned as the maximum achievable element.
5:java:6: Sorting dominates the cost, giving an overall time complexity of n times log n, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
5:cpp:1: Sorting the vector from beginning to end in ascending order orders the vector from smallest to largest, which lets the greedy pass below limit each element as gently as possible using its preceding element.
5:cpp:2: The variable prev is set to zero, representing the value before the first position; by capping the first actual element at prev plus one, which equals one, the required rule that the first element equals one is enforced automatically.
5:cpp:3: The range based for loop iterates num over the sorted vector in increasing order.
5:cpp:4: Setting prev to the smaller of the current number and one more than the previous value assigns this position the largest possible value that still respects the original value as a ceiling, meaning values can only decrease, while also keeping the difference from the previous position no more than one.
5:cpp:5: After processing every element, prev holds the value assigned to the last position, which is then returned as the maximum achievable element.
5:cpp:6: Sorting dominates the cost, giving an overall time complexity of order of n times log of n, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
6:javascript:1: The call to products dot sort orders every product name in lexicographic order, since JavaScript’s default sort is lexicographic for strings, so products that share a prefix appear consecutively.
6:javascript:2: The result gathers the suggestion list for every keystroke, and the prefix keeps track of the characters typed so far.
6:javascript:3: The for…of loop iterates over each character of searchWord, simulating one keystroke at a time.
6:javascript:4: The prefix is increased by adding the newly typed character to it.
6:javascript:5: A manual binary search narrows the low and high bounds until they converge on the first index where the product is not less than the prefix, using the standard mid-point comparison and half-interval elimination.
6:javascript:6: Starting at lo, the code scans forward through at most three products and keeps only the ones that pass the starts with prefix check, because not every product at that index necessarily shares the prefix.
6:javascript:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:javascript:8: Once all characters have been processed, the result is returned.
6:javascript:9: Sorting takes on the order of p times the log of p for p products, and each of up to m keystrokes performs on the order of the log of p time for a binary search plus constant time for scanning, for an overall time complexity on the order of p times the log of p plus m times the log of p.
6:python:1: Sorting the products list orders every product name in lexicographical order, which is required so that products sharing a common prefix appear next to each other and the three lexicographically smallest matches are simply the first three encountered.
6:python:2: The result gathers the list of suggestions for each character typed, and the prefix stores the characters entered so far, beginning as an empty string.
6:python:3: The for loop goes through each character of the search word in order, simulating one keystroke at a time.
6:python:4: Prefix plus equals see, extending the prefix by the newly typed character.
6:python:5: The bisect bisect left function binary searches the sorted list of products for the first index where the product is not less than the prefix, which is exactly where any product starting with the prefix would first appear.
6:python:6: Starting at that index, the code scans forward across up to three products, limited by the smaller of i plus three and the total length of the products list, and it keeps only the products that actually begin with the prefix, because some products near that index may not share the prefix at all.
6:python:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:python:8: Once every character has been processed, the result is returned, containing a suggestion list for each prefix length.
6:python:9: Sorting takes time proportional to p times the logarithm of p for p products, and each of up to m keystrokes does work proportional to the logarithm of p for a binary search plus constant-time scanning, for an overall time complexity proportional to p times the logarithm of p plus m times the logarithm of p.
6:java:1: The sort routine orders every product name in lexicographic order, so products that share a prefix appear next to each other.
6:java:2: The result collects the suggestion list for each keystroke, and the prefix, a StringBuilder chosen to avoid repeated string concatenation, accumulates the characters that have been typed so far.
6:java:3: The for loop goes through each character of the search word one by one, in order.
6:java:4: Prefix dot append takes a character and extends the prefix, and p stores the prefix’s current string value so it can be used for the comparisons that follow.
6:java:5: A manual binary search narrows the lower and upper bounds using compare to against p until they converge on the first index whose product is not lexicographically less than p.
6:java:6: Starting at lo, the code scans forward up to three products and keeps only the ones whose starts with p check passes.
6:java:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:java:8: Once every character has been processed, the result is returned.
6:java:9: Sorting costs proportional to p times the logarithm of p for p products, and each of up to m keystrokes performs work proportional to the logarithm of p for a binary search plus a constant amount for scanning, for an overall time complexity proportional to p times the logarithm of p plus m times the logarithm of p.
6:cpp:1: The command sorts the products list by ordering every product name in lexicographical order, so that products with the same starting prefix appear next to each other.
6:cpp:2: The result gathers the suggestion list for each keystroke, and the prefix accumulates the characters typed so far, starting as empty.
6:cpp:3: The range-based for loop goes through the characters of search word one by one in order, assigning each character to ch.
6:cpp:4: Prefix adds the newly typed character to the end of the existing prefix.
6:cpp:5: The lower bound function on the sorted products vector and the prefix performs a binary search to find the first element that is not less than the prefix, and it returns the index where any product whose name starts with the prefix would first appear.
6:cpp:6: Starting at that index, the code scans forward for up to three products, bounded by both the size of the vector and the value of lo plus three, and it keeps only the ones that pass the compare of zero with the prefix length against the prefix, confirming they actually start with the prefix.
6:cpp:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:cpp:8: Once every character has been processed, the result is returned.
6:cpp:9: Sorting the p products costs on the order of p times the log of p, and each of the up to m keystrokes performs a binary search that takes on the order of the log of p plus an additional scan that takes constant time, so the overall time complexity is on the order of p times the log of p plus m times the log of p.
7:javascript:1: bCount keeps track of how many b characters have been seen so far, and deletions tracks the minimum number of deletions needed to make the string balanced up through the current position; both begin at zero.
7:javascript:2: The for dot dot of loop goes through c for every character of s, one after another in order.
7:javascript:3: Whenever c is the letter b, bCount is increased, because that b might later need to be deleted if an a comes after it.
7:javascript:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the code compares the number of deletions plus one, meaning it deletes this single a, against b count, meaning it deletes every b seen so far, and it keeps the smaller value as the new running total of deletions.
7:javascript:5: After scanning the entire string, deletions is returned as the minimum number of deletions needed to remove every b that occurs before some a.
7:javascript:6: The function makes one pass over the string s, doing the same amount of work for each character in constant time, which gives linear time in the length of s, and constant extra space.
7:python:1: b_count keeps track of how many b characters have been seen so far, and deletions keeps track of the minimum number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position; both start at zero.
7:python:2: The for loop iterates c over each character of s, in order.
7:python:3: Whenever c is b, b_count is increased, because that b might need to be deleted if an a appears later.
7:python:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the string is unbalanced if any b comes before this a, so the code chooses the cheaper of two ways to fix it: deletions plus one means deleting this single a instead, while b count means deleting every b seen so far instead; min picks whichever cost is lower, and this becomes the new running deletions total.
7:python:5: After scanning the whole string, the deletions variable holds the minimum number of deletions needed to remove every b that comes before some a, which is exactly the condition for balance.
7:python:6: The algorithm makes a single pass over the string s, doing only a constant amount of work for each character, resulting in an overall time complexity that grows linearly with n and using only constant extra space.
7:java:1: bCount keeps track of how many b characters have been seen so far, and deletions keeps track of the minimum number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position; both begin at zero.
7:java:2: The enhanced for loop iterates c over every character of the array produced by calling s to convert it to a character array, in order.
7:java:3: Whenever the current character is b, bCount is increased, because that b might later have to be deleted if an a comes after it.
7:java:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the code compares the cost of deleting one a, meaning deletions plus one, against b count, meaning the cost of deleting every b seen so far, and uses the smaller value as the new running total of deletions.
7:java:5: After scanning the entire string, the algorithm returns the minimum number of deletions required.
7:java:6: The method makes a single pass over s, taking linear time, and uses constant extra space.
7:cpp:1: bCount keeps track of how many letter b characters have been seen so far, and deletions keeps track of the minimum number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position; both start at zero.
7:cpp:2: The range based for loop iterates c over every character of s, one after the other in order.
7:cpp:3: Whenever c is the letter b, b count is increased, because that b might later need to be deleted if an a appears after it.
7:cpp:4: Whenever the current character c is the letter a, the code compares the cost of deleting this single a plus one against the cost of deleting every b seen so far, using the minimum of the two, and it keeps the smaller cost as the new running total of deletions.
7:cpp:5: After scanning the entire string, deletions is returned as the minimum number of deletions needed.
7:cpp:6: The function makes a single pass over s, taking linear time and using constant extra space.
8:javascript:1: It starts at the head, and the for loop advances it k minus one times, ending on the k-th node from the beginning, using one-based indexing.
8:javascript:2: The front saves a reference to the node that is k positions from the start, so its value can be changed later.
8:javascript:3: Second also starts at the head, and the node variable is set to the same position as the first pointer, so both pointers begin the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:javascript:4: The while loop moves node forward one step at a time until node’s next pointer is null, meaning node reaches the last node, while moving second forward in step on every iteration; since node started at the k-th node, this moves second forward exactly length minus k steps, landing it on the k-th node from the end.
8:javascript:5: A temporary variable is used to swap only the value fields of the front and second nodes, while leaving every next pointer in the list completely unchanged.
8:javascript:6: The head is returned unchanged, because only the values were swapped and the list’s structure was never modified.
8:javascript:7: The function makes two passes over at most the entire list, taking linear time and using constant extra space.
8:python:1: First, it starts at the head, and the for loop advances it by k minus one times, landing it on the k-th node from the beginning, counting from one.
8:python:2: The front stores a reference to the k-th node from the beginning so it can be modified later.
8:python:3: The second pointer also starts at the head, and the node is set to the same position as the first pointer, so both pointers begin the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:python:4: The while loop advances the first node one step at a time until the next pointer of the first node is none, meaning the first node reaches the last node, while advancing the second node in lockstep on every iteration; because the first node started at the k-th node and the second node started at the head, this moves the second node forward exactly length minus k steps, so it lands on the k-th node from the end.
8:python:5: The statement front dot val, second dot val equals second dot val, front dot val swaps only the values stored in the two identified nodes, leaving the list’s structure, meaning the next pointers, completely unchanged.
8:python:6: The head is returned unchanged, because only the values were swapped and the list’s overall structure was never modified.
8:python:7: The solution makes two passes over at most the full list, so it runs in linear time, using constant extra space, and it does not need to know the list length in advance.
8:java:1: It first starts at the head, and the loop advances it k minus one times, landing it on the k-th node from the beginning, using one-based indexing.
8:java:2: The front keeps a reference to the node that is k positions from the beginning, so its value can be changed later.
8:java:3: Second also starts at the head, and the node is set to the same position as first, so both pointers begin the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:java:4: The while loop advances the first node one step at a time until the next pointer of the first node is null, meaning the first node has reached the last node, while advancing the second node in lockstep on every iteration; since the first node started at the k-th node, this moves the second node exactly length minus k steps forward, landing it on the k-th node from the end.
8:java:5: A temporary variable is used to swap only the value fields of the front and second nodes, while leaving every other reference in the list completely unchanged.
8:java:6: The head is returned unchanged, because the algorithm only swaps values and never modifies the shape or structure of the list.
8:java:7: The method makes two passes over the list at most, giving linear time and constant extra space.
8:cpp:1: First, it starts at the head, and the loop advances it k minus one times, so it lands on the k-th node from the beginning, using one-based indexing.
8:cpp:2: The front part saves a pointer to the node that is k positions from the start, so that its value can be changed later.
8:cpp:3: Second also begins at the head, and the node is placed at the same position as first, so both pointers start the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:cpp:4: The while loop moves the first node forward one step at a time until the next pointer of node is null, meaning node has reached the last node, while moving the second node forward in sync on every iteration; since the second node started at the k-th node, it advances exactly the number of steps equal to the length minus k, and so it lands on the k-th node from the end.
8:cpp:5: Swapping the values in the two identified nodes exchanges only their val fields and leaves every next pointer in the list completely unchanged.
8:cpp:6: The head is returned unchanged, because only the values were swapped and the list’s structure was never altered.
8:cpp:7: The function makes two passes over at most the entire list, taking linear time and using constant extra space.
9:javascript:1: n stores the array length, and best keeps track of the length of the longest balanced subarray found so far, starting at zero.
9:javascript:2: The outer loop goes through i for every possible starting position of a subarray.
9:javascript:3: Evens and odds are new Sets created for each new starting index i, since the counts of distinct values have to be computed separately for every starting position.
9:javascript:4: The inner for loop iterates j from i to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right at a time.
9:javascript:5: Each new element from nums at index j is added to the evens collection if it is even, or to the odds collection if it is odd; the Set automatically removes duplicate values, so each Set’s size reflects only distinct values.
9:javascript:6: Whenever the size of the even numbers equals the size of the odd numbers, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so best is updated to the larger of its current value and the subarray length, which is j minus i plus one.
9:javascript:7: After every pair of starting and ending indices has been checked, the best result returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:javascript:8: Because every pair of start and end indices is examined using set operations that take constant time on average, the function runs in quadratic time and uses linear extra space, which is acceptable given that nums length is at most fifteen hundred.
9:python:1: n stores the array length, and best tracks the length of the longest balanced subarray found so far, starting at zero.
9:python:2: The outer loop runs i through every possible starting position for a subarray.
9:python:3: The even and odd sets are fresh each time, reset for every new starting index i, because the distinct value counts for a subarray must be computed independently for every starting point.
9:python:4: The inner for loop iterates j from i to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right each time.
9:python:5: Each new element from nums at index j is added to the evens set if it is even, or to the odds set if it is odd, and using sets automatically removes duplicates, so each set’s size counts only distinct values.
9:python:6: Whenever the number of distinct even numbers equals the number of distinct odd numbers, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so the best value is updated to the larger of its current value and the subarray’s length, which is j minus i plus one.
9:python:7: After every starting index and ending index combination has been checked, the best value returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:python:8: Because every pair of start and end indices is examined using constant-time amortized set operations, the algorithm runs in quadratic time and uses linear extra space, which is acceptable given the constraint that nums length is at most fifteen hundred.
9:java:1: n stores the length of the array, and best tracks the length of the longest balanced subarray found so far, starting from zero.
9:java:2: The outer loop goes through every possible starting position i for a subarray.
9:java:3: For each new starting index i, create fresh hash sets for the even and odd values, because the number of distinct values has to be computed independently for every starting point.
9:java:4: The inner loop iterates j from i up to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right each time.
9:java:5: Each new element of nums at position j is added to the evens set if it is even, or to the odds set if it is odd; the hash set automatically removes duplicates for repeated values, so the size of each set reflects only the distinct values.
9:java:6: Whenever the number of even elements is the same as the number of odd elements, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so the best value is updated to the larger of its current value and the subarray length, which is j minus i plus one.
9:java:7: After every possible starting index and ending index combination has been checked, the best result returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:java:8: Because every pair of starting and ending indices is examined and each step uses constant time amortized set operations, the method runs in quadratic time and uses linear extra space, which is acceptable given that the length of the input array is at most fifteen hundred.
9:cpp:1: n stores the array size, and best tracks the length of the longest balanced subarray found so far, starting at zero.
9:cpp:2: The outer for loop goes through every possible starting index of a subarray, using the variable i.
9:cpp:3: For each new starting index i, create fresh unordered sets for the even and odd values, because the counts of distinct values must be computed independently for every starting point.
9:cpp:4: The inner loop iterates j starting from i up to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right each time.
9:cpp:5: Each new element from nums at position j is added into the set of evens if it is even, or into the set of odds if it is odd. The unordered_set automatically removes duplicates, so each set’s size counts only distinct values.
9:cpp:6: Whenever the number of even elements equals the number of odd elements, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so best is updated to the larger of its current value and the subarray length, meaning j minus i plus one.
9:cpp:7: After every possible starting and ending index pair has been checked, the best value returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:cpp:8: Because every pair of starting and ending indices is considered, using constant time amortized set operations, the function runs in time proportional to n squared and uses extra space proportional to n, which is acceptable given that nums size is at most fifteen hundred.
10:javascript:1: A Map counts how many times each different value appears in nums, increasing the stored count for that value, starting from zero if it has not been seen before, for each element during one pass through nums.
10:javascript:2: Buckets are created as an array containing nums dot length plus one empty arrays, with one array for every possible frequency from zero up to nums dot length, because no value can appear more times than the total length of the array.
10:javascript:3: For every distinct number and its frequency, the number is pushed into the bucket named buckets for that frequency, effectively performing a bucket sort where the bucket index is the frequency itself rather than the value.
10:javascript:4: The result collects the final answer as numbers are gathered.
10:javascript:5: The outer loop goes through the frequencies from the highest possible frequency down to one, and it stops early as soon as the length of the result reaches k.
10:javascript:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is added to the result, and the inner loop is stopped as soon as the result size reaches k, so no unnecessary work is done.
10:javascript:7: The result is returned once we have collected enough of the most frequent elements.
10:javascript:8: Building the frequency map and the buckets takes time proportional to n, and scanning the buckets also takes time proportional to n in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is proportional to n, which satisfies the follow up requirement of beating time proportional to n log n.
10:python:1: Counter on nums counts how many times each distinct value appears, doing it in one pass.
10:python:2: Buckets is a list of length one more than nums, consisting of empty lists for every possible frequency from zero up to the length of nums, because no value can appear more times than the array’s total length.
10:python:3: For every distinct number and its frequency, the number is appended to the bucket associated with that frequency, effectively performing a bucket sort where the bucket index is the frequency rather than the value.
10:python:4: The result gathers the final answer as numbers are collected.
10:python:5: The outer for loop walks frequency downward from the highest possible frequency to one, visiting buckets in decreasing order of frequency.
10:python:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is added to the result, because they all have the same frequency and are equally valid candidates for the top k.
10:python:7: Once the result reaches length k, it is returned immediately, so the scan stops early as soon as enough of the most frequent elements have been collected.
10:python:8: If every bucket is used up without an early return—this can only happen if k equals the total number of distinct values—then the result is returned as a fallback.
10:python:9: Building the frequency counts and buckets takes time proportional to n, and scanning the buckets also takes time proportional to n in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is proportional to n, which satisfies the follow-up requirement of improving on time proportional to n log n.
10:java:1: A HashMap counts how many times each different value appears in the input array, and it uses merge with Integer dot sum to increase each running count in a single pass.
10:java:2: Buckets is built as a list containing nums dot length plus one empty lists, with one list for every possible frequency from zero up to nums dot length, since no value can appear more times than the array’s total length.
10:java:3: For each distinct number and its frequency, the number is added to the bucket indicated by that frequency, using buckets dot get with the entry’s frequency, which effectively performs a bucket sort where the bucket index is the frequency itself.
10:java:4: The result is a fixed-size array of length k, which is the exact number of elements to return, and idx keeps track of how many slots have been filled.
10:java:5: The outer loop counts frequency down from the highest possible value to one, and it stops early once the index reaches k.
10:java:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is copied into result at the current index, then the index is increased, and it leaves the inner loop as soon as the index reaches k.
10:java:7: The result is returned once k elements have been collected, and they are filled in descending order of frequency.
10:java:8: Building the frequency map and the buckets takes linear time, and scanning the buckets also takes linear time in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is linear, which satisfies the follow up requirement of beating linearithmic time.
10:cpp:1: An unordered map tallies how many times each distinct value appears in nums, by incrementing the count for that value for every element in a single pass.
10:cpp:2: The buckets array is created with nums size plus one empty vectors, with one vector for every possible frequency from zero up to nums size, because no value can occur more times than the total length of the array.
10:cpp:3: For each distinct number and its frequency, the number is placed into buckets at the position given by that frequency, effectively performing a bucket sort where the bucket index is the frequency itself.
10:cpp:4: The result gathers the final answer as numbers are collected.
10:cpp:5: The outer loop walks the frequency downward from the highest possible frequency down to one, and it stops early as soon as the result size reaches k.
10:cpp:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is added to the result list, and the inner loop stops as soon as the result list size reaches k.
10:cpp:7: The result is returned as soon as we have gathered a sufficient number of the most frequent elements.
10:cpp:8: Building the frequency map and the buckets takes time proportional to n, and scanning the buckets also takes time proportional to n in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is proportional to n, which satisfies the follow-up requirement of beating time proportional to n log n.
